repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
Implements `:packu[pdate][!] [++offline] [++lockfile] [name]` and  `:packd[el][!] ++all`/`:packd[el][!] {name}`.

Still up for debate whether these should be built-in commands (`:packupdate`, `:packdel`) or user commands (`:PackUpdate`, `:PackDelete`). I've implemented them as built-in commands for the time being, but I can easily change them if a different decision is made.

Closes #34764.

cc @echasnovski

comment_data.body:
```suggestion
  local res = {}
  for _, plug_data in ipairs(vim.pack.get(nil, { info = false })) do
    if not filter or filter(plug_data) then
      res[#res + 1] = plug_data.spec.name
    end
  end
  return res
```

I think `vim.iter` here is a bit too much. An explicit loop is more expressive and concise.

Plus passing `{ info = false }` for this case avoids gathering redundant Git information from the plugins.

comment_data.body:
I am generally fine with not showing a warning here (it is important for confirmation buffer, though), but it is confusing to see different behavior for a function and a command. I'd expect they act in the same way here. For example, both do nothing silently or show an error. If to choose, I'd prefer the first one.

@justinmk, does this (silent for Lua function, error for command) fit a general design?

comment_data.body: I did this because when these error messages are displayed is different for the interactive commands verses the lua functions. For lua, these warnings where printed when an empty list was passed. But for `:packupdate` and `:packdel`, it is impossible to pass an empty list: `:packup` by itself updates all packages, and `:packdel` by itself is an error. Thus, the only way for `E5808` and `E5810` to appear is for there to be no plugins installed, or in `:packdel` case, for all plugins to be active.

comment_data.body:
This kind of complex parameter which takes a value (`++target=lockfile`) is where the use-case starts to get questionable. It's risky to introduce, or amplify, that precedent. 

Those kinds of parameters are much better suited for the programmatic interface, `vim.pack.update()`. (Historical note: `:syntax` and `:hi` have such parameters likely as a way to avoid `FEAT_EVAL` in Vim. They were a mistake...)

If "target=lockfile" is a reasonably common interactive use-case, I would just suggest providing it as a more ergonimc name instead of a `key=value`.

comment_data.body:
1. Returning `false` if the condition is not met for some plugins is technically different from [how `vim.pack.del()` does things](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/blob/6780e78b0981f52aec9c4ef96356d3fef3742c9a/runtime/lua/vim/pack.lua#L1378-L1390). The "do as much as possible before reporting an error" approach here seems to be more useful.
2. There is no indication in the helper signature that `allow_active` is for deleting, but the message hard codes "deleted".

I think it might be better to either split or repurpose this helper so that it can perform the same as `vim.pack.del()`.

Or to indeed go back prior to [this comment](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/39693#discussion_r3230496727), but use as similar wording as possible and choose between `nvim_echo` / `error()`.

comment_data.body:
The `vim.tbl_keys`  does not guarantee the order of the keys. Having completion candidates be in useful order seems important. The `vim.pack.get()` already has the order of "first active in order they were `vim.pack.add`ed and then inactive in alphabetical". Not sure if it is a good fit for completion candidates, which is probably better to just be alphabetical.

So on top of [this comment](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/39693/changes#r3240281144), maybe just doing `local res = vim.tbl_keys(...); table.sort(res); return res` is the best approach here.

comment_data.body: I disagree that "do as much as possible before reporting an error" is the correct way to go here. I think invalid input should be disregarded without side-effects, instead of doing some stuff and then failing. However, I admit that `:packdel` and `vim.pack.del()` behaving differently in this regard is unideal, so I'll change this back. I'll address possible changes in a separate PR where we can change the behavior of both `:packdel` and `vim.pack.del()` at once.

comment_regular_data.body:
Thanks for working on this!

Couple of thoughts for posterity:

- Not having a command for `vim.pack.add()` is... both fine and a bit awkward. Fine because it is indeed primarily designed to be put into 'init.lua'. A bit awkward because not every user-facing `vim.pack` functionality is covered with commands. No strong opinion here.

- Having `:packupdate` and `:packdelete` be for `vim.pack` while `:packadd` is not is... both fine and a bit awkward. Mostly for this reason I prefer uppercase user commands like `:Pack{Add,Update,Del}` or a separate `:pack {add,update,del}`. But the first seems to go against the overall design while the second is technically a breaking change (`:pack` now means `:packadd` as a valid unique abbreviation) and using `!` is a bit more awkward (`:pack! update`).

I guess improving `:packadd` to allow `++src`, `++name`, and `++version` will partially cover both above points, but also no strong opinion here.

> or a separate `:pack {add,update,del}`.

yes, i thought that's what was decided...

> is technically a breaking change (`:pack` now means `:packadd` as a valid unique abbreviation

i think that is a solvable problem, there are plenty of commands whose abbrevs are "grandfathered". we can also just decide to "break" that abbreviation.

> > or a separate `:pack {add,update,del}`.
> 
> yes, i thought that's what was decided...

I considered that originally, but I decided against that for the following reasons:
- `:pack! update` and `:pack! delete` is a pattern I *really* don't want to create. Not only does it look bad (imo), but the `!` has a different meaning depending on the subcommand ("skip confirmation" vs. "allow inactive plugins"). The alternative would be seperate `++force`/`++active` arguments, but those take much longer to type than a simple `!`.
- To me, `:packupdate` and `:packdelete` feel much more separate than the subcommands for `:lsp` and `:syntax` do. Especially when these commands take different arguments (`:packupdate` has `++offline` and `++target=`, while `:packdelete` has `++all`).
- I don't foresee us needing to add any additional commands/subcommands for `vim.pack`.
That's a great explanation, let's include it in the commit msg for future reference :)

Ready for review!

I'm unhappy with the docs' formatting as it currently is, but it's the best I could do with `@brief`. As followup work, it would be great to have `vim.pack`'s main docs not be generated from a lua file, similar to the docs for `vim.lsp` and others.

I also slightly changed the behavior of `vim.pack.update()` and `vim.pack.del()`: they now accept an empty list of plugin names without printing a warning. Now that we have dedicated interactive commands, interactive messages like these are just noisy when used in scripts.

> eh, we want to go the other direction, since the generator tends to produce better formatting. though in this case it sounds like there was some issue... but that is just something we want to fix in the generator.

Got it! The generator will need some new feature then. It needs the ability to escape `[` and `]` characters without breaking (and do block indentation if that's also unsupported), and then `@brief` will work perfectly.

Another benefit on having `:packupdate` as a standalone command (builtin or user command) instead of `:pack update` is that it is likely to be a more popular command to run and being able to fuzzy pick it in one go is a plus

> Another benefit of `:packupdate` instead of `:pack update` is ... being able to fuzzy pick it in one go

Good point. I think "sub commands" may actually be an antipattern in most cases. [Noted.](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/32263#issuecomment-4436002808)
